explicit_text: I was born in Yamabuki, Shimoina-Gun, Nagano-Ken, Japan in 1890. My father was the principal of the Yamabuki grammar school there, and although he valued education highly, our family was poor and I was denied the chance to pursue schooling during my early years. Life in Yamabuki was difficult, especially after my mother passed away when I was two, and I was raised by my maternal grandmother.
implicit_text: I was born in a small mountain village in Shimoina-Gun, Nagano-Ken, Japan in 1890. My father was the principal of the local grammar school there, and although he valued education highly, our family was poor and I was denied the chance to pursue schooling during my early years. Life in that village was difficult, especially after my mother passed away when I was two, and I was raised by my maternal grandmother.

explicit_text: Japan was my homeland where I spent my childhood and early adulthood. After my mother died, my family was separated, and I eventually moved to Nara with my stepmother and brother. My father left Japan to study in America, and later I returned to Japan briefly before moving to the United States in 1915. The customs and traditions of Japan, including arranged marriage, shaped much of my early life.

explicit_text: When I was about 12 years old, I moved to Nara to live with my stepmother and brother. It was my first time traveling by train and I was awed by the city compared to my mountain village. Although the promise of education was not immediate, I later attended Giei Gakko in nearby Gose and graduated with honors after four years of study.

explicit_text: I came to the United States in September 1915 to join my husband, who had returned from his studies in America. Upon arrival, I was welcomed warmly and introduced to a church community where I was baptized. My life here was filled with both joy and hardship, including personal struggles and later the challenges of wartime internment. Despite difficulties, the U.S. became my home for many decades.

explicit_text: Yohei Tanaka was a young man who had just been baptized when he kindly offered to take me to church after I arrived in the United States. From that day until his death in 1973, his family acted as constant guardians for mine. He played an important role in helping me adjust to my new faith and life in America.

explicit_text: Rev. Kiyama was a minister from Japan who gave a sermon titled 'The Joy of Belief in Jesus Christ' that profoundly changed my life. I met him during a very difficult time after my separation from my husband, and his message based on John 3:16 helped me find true faith and redemption. That Sunday marked a spiritual rebirth for me and set the course for my future.

explicit_text: Mr. Gammier was a renowned French designer who took me on as an apprentice when I decided to pursue sewing and design to support myself. Under his expert guidance, I refined my skills over two years and was encouraged by him to open my own shop. However, due to racial tensions in Southern California, I chose instead to work for Madame Alma, with Mr. Gammier's understanding and support.

explicit_text: In 1941, following the outbreak of war in the Pacific, my family and I were first taken to the Tanforan Assembly Center as part of the forced relocation of Japanese Americans. This was a painful and shameful time, as we were separated from our friends and community. The experience marked the beginning of a difficult chapter in my life during the Japanese American internment.

explicit_text: After Tanforan, we were relocated to the Topaz War Relocation Center in the desert of Utah. The harsh desert climate was a stark contrast to the mild environment of San Francisco, and adjusting was very challenging. I taught sewing classes there while my husband worked in the camp infirmary, and we found moments of beauty like watching the brilliant sunsets together.

explicit_text: San Francisco was my home before the war and where my husband and I returned after being released from the internment camps in 1945. Upon return, we found our homes occupied and properties lost, forcing us to share living quarters with other displaced families. Despite these hardships, we rebuilt our lives, with my husband reopening his practice and us renting a house within ten months.

explicit_text: The Japanese American internment during World War II was a deeply painful period in my life. In 1941, my family and I were forcibly removed from our home and sent first to Tanforan Assembly Center and then to Topaz War Relocation Center in Utah. Despite the injustice and hardship, I maintained my faith and found ways to support others, such as teaching sewing classes. Returning to San Francisco after the war, we faced the loss of our home and property but worked hard to rebuild our lives.
